_comment: Bugcheck (BSOD stop-code) lookup. Keyed by normalized hex matching Invoke-SecondOpinion.ps1 Format-Bugcheck (0x## when <=0xFF, else 0x###...). Fields: name, class (scorer vocab: hardware|memory|storage|gpu|cpu|driver|software|power|mixed), hint, search. Built 2026-06-22 from Microsoft Learn bug-check reference + community heuristics via the kb-build workflow.

0x0A.name: IRQL_NOT_LESS_OR_EQUAL
0x0A.hint: Kernel code touched memory at too high an interrupt level, almost always a buggy driver or bad RAM; same driver named every crash points to the driver, varied names suggest memory.

0x18.name: REFERENCE_BY_POINTER
0x18.hint: A driver mismanaged an object's reference count (released it once too often), corrupting kernel object state; update or remove the driver named in the dump.

0x1A.name: MEMORY_MANAGEMENT
0x1A.hint: A severe memory-management fault; some parameter codes point at faulty RAM while others point at a buggy driver, so run Windows Memory Diagnostic first but treat varied parameters as a hardware hint.

0x1E.name: KMODE_EXCEPTION_NOT_HANDLED
0x1E.hint: A kernel-mode program hit an error it could not handle, usually a faulty driver; the named module in the crash points to the culprit, and a recurring module means a software/driver fault.

0x22.name: FILE_SYSTEM
0x22.hint: A generic, rarely-seen file-system driver fault pointing at the storage stack; run !analyze, check Event Viewer for disk errors, and verify drive health with CHKDSK.

0x23.name: FAT_FILE_SYSTEM
0x23.hint: A fault in the FAT/FASTFAT file-system driver, usually disk corruption or bad sectors on a FAT-formatted volume (common on USB/SD media); run CHKDSK /f /r on the affected drive.

0x24.name: NTFS_FILE_SYSTEM
0x24.hint: A fault in ntfs.sys, usually from disk corruption or bad sectors on an NTFS drive; if every crash names ntfs.sys it leans disk-corruption, so run CHKDSK and a drive self-test.

0x3B.name: SYSTEM_SERVICE_EXCEPTION
0x3B.hint: An exception hit while code crossed from user mode into the kernel, typically a driver dereferencing a bad/NULL pointer; a consistently named driver points to software, varied modules hint at memory.

0x4D.name: NO_PAGES_AVAILABLE
0x4D.hint: No free physical pages remain, typically because a driver is leaking memory or holding too many pages locked; a single driver consistently consuming pages points to a software leak, not bad RAM.

0x4E.name: PFN_LIST_CORRUPT
0x4E.hint: The page-frame-number list is corrupted, usually because a driver passed a bad memory descriptor list, but bad RAM can also cause it; a consistent culprit driver across crashes points to software.

0x50.name: PAGE_FAULT_IN_NONPAGED_AREA
0x50.hint: Invalid or already-freed system memory was referenced; usually a faulty driver or bad RAM, though a corrupted NTFS volume is a documented cause, so same driver named = software, varied = test RAM and disk.

0x76.name: PROCESS_HAS_LOCKED_PAGES
0x76.hint: A driver failed to release memory pages it locked for I/O when a process ended; enable TrackLockedPages or check the dump to find the leaking driver, then update it.

0x77.name: KERNEL_STACK_INPAGE_ERROR
0x77.hint: A kernel-stack page could not be read from disk, pointing to bad blocks, loose cabling, or a failing drive (sometimes bad RAM); status 0xC000009C/0xC000016A in parameter 2 means bad sectors.

0x7A.name: KERNEL_DATA_INPAGE_ERROR
0x7A.hint: Kernel data could not be paged in from disk; parameter 2 usually names the cause (0xC000009C/0xC000016A = bad sectors, 0xC000009D = loose cabling), with failing RAM as a secondary suspect.

0x7B.name: INACCESSIBLE_BOOT_DEVICE
0x7B.hint: Windows lost access to the system partition at startup, typically a boot-drive failure or wrong storage-controller driver (e.g. an AHCI-vs-RAID BIOS mode change); appears right at boot before logon.

0x7E.name: SYSTEM_THREAD_EXCEPTION_NOT_HANDLED
0x7E.hint: A system thread generated an error nobody handled, typically an incompatible or corrupt driver named on the blue screen; a consistent module means update or roll back that driver.

0x7F.name: UNEXPECTED_KERNEL_MODE_TRAP
0x7F.hint: The CPU generated a trap the kernel could not handle (often a double fault from stack overflow); a hardware-flavored trap code commonly indicates faulty RAM or a failing CPU/motherboard.

0x80.name: NMI_HARDWARE_FAILURE
0x80.hint: A non-maskable interrupt signalled a hardware malfunction hard to pin down; suspect failing RAM, motherboard, or other physical hardware and test components one at a time.

0x8E.name: KERNEL_MODE_EXCEPTION_NOT_HANDLED
0x8E.hint: An unhandled kernel error, frequently a driver or failing hardware; if the same module repeats it is a driver, but random codes alongside it often mean bad RAM.

0x9C.name: MACHINE_CHECK_EXCEPTION
0x9C.hint: The CPU raised a fatal machine-check exception (the legacy form of 0x124, mainly on older hardware), pointing at the processor, overclock, voltage, or thermal problems.

0x9F.name: DRIVER_POWER_STATE_FAILURE
0x9F.hint: A driver failed to complete a power transition (often sleep or wake) in time, usually a network, USB, storage, or GPU driver; the device blocking the power request is named in the dump.

0xA0.name: INTERNAL_POWER_ERROR
0xA0.hint: The Windows power policy manager hit a fatal error, often during sleep/hibernate transitions; leads are power/chipset/battery drivers, a corrupt hiberfil, or buggy BIOS/ACPI firmware.

0xA2.name: MEMORY_IMAGE_CORRUPT
0xA2.hint: The memory manager detected corruption in a loaded image's in-memory pages; because resident image corruption is often physical, faulty RAM is a prime suspect alongside a driver illegally writing memory.

0xAD.name: VIDEO_DRIVER_DEBUG_REPORT_REQUEST
0xAD.hint: Not a true crash but a non-fatal minidump the video port created because the GPU driver requested a debug report; updating the graphics driver is the lead if these recur.

0xB4.name: VIDEO_DRIVER_INIT_FAILURE
0xB4.hint: Windows could not enter graphics mode because no display miniport driver would start, pointing to a missing, corrupt, or incompatible GPU driver; boot Safe Mode and reinstall the display driver.

0xBE.name: ATTEMPTED_WRITE_TO_READONLY_MEMORY
0xBE.hint: A driver tried to write into a memory region marked read-only; the offending driver name is usually shown on the blue screen, so update or remove that driver.

0xC1.name: SPECIAL_POOL_DETECTED_MEMORY_CORRUPTION
0xC1.hint: Driver Verifier's special pool caught a driver writing outside its allocation; the backtrace of the current thread usually names the offending driver, and it appears mainly with Verifier enabled.

0xC2.name: BAD_POOL_CALLER
0xC2.hint: A driver made an illegal pool request (such as freeing the same block twice or freeing an unallocated address); a recurring driver name across crashes confirms the software culprit.

0xC4.name: DRIVER_VERIFIER_DETECTED_VIOLATION
0xC4.hint: The general Driver Verifier stop code raised when Verifier catches a driver breaking the rules; the violating driver is named in the dump, so update or remove it.

0xC5.name: DRIVER_CORRUPTED_EXPOOL
0xC5.hint: The system touched invalid memory at high IRQL because a driver corrupted the system pool (small allocation); run Driver Verifier's special pool to name the consistent culprit driver.

0xC6.name: DRIVER_CAUGHT_MODIFYING_FREED_POOL
0xC6.hint: Driver Verifier caught a driver writing to pool memory it had already freed (a use-after-free); the dump's backtrace names the responsible module.

0xC9.name: DRIVER_VERIFIER_IOMANAGER_VIOLATION
0xC9.hint: Driver Verifier's I/O checks caught a driver mishandling I/O requests; the offending driver is named in the crash, making this a clear driver fault to update or remove.

0xCE.name: DRIVER_UNLOADED_WITHOUT_CANCELLING_PENDING_OPERATIONS
0xCE.hint: A driver unloaded while leaving timers, worker threads, or callbacks active, which then ran into freed memory; the responsible driver is named on the blue screen, so update or remove it.

0xD0.name: DRIVER_CORRUPTED_MMPOOL
0xD0.hint: Like 0xC5 but for a large allocation: a driver corrupted the memory-manager pool and the system accessed invalid memory at high IRQL; identify the driver with Driver Verifier's special pool.

0xD1.name: DRIVER_IRQL_NOT_LESS_OR_EQUAL
0xD1.hint: A kernel-mode driver accessed pageable or invalid memory at too high an interrupt level, the classic bad-driver crash; the driver named in the dump is the direct lead.

0xD5.name: DRIVER_PAGE_FAULT_IN_FREED_SPECIAL_POOL
0xD5.hint: A driver touched memory it had already freed, caught by Special Pool diagnostics; the named driver is the cause, usually surfaced while running Driver Verifier.

0xD6.name: DRIVER_PAGE_FAULT_BEYOND_END_OF_ALLOCATION
0xD6.hint: A driver read or wrote past the end of its memory buffer (a buffer overrun), caught by Special Pool; the named driver is the lead to update or remove.

0xEA.name: THREAD_STUCK_IN_DEVICE_DRIVER
0xEA.hint: A driver thread is stuck spinning forever waiting on hardware, frequently a bad video card or display driver; the driver name is in parameter 3, same module repeating points to the driver, varied to a failing GPU.

0xED.name: UNMOUNTABLE_BOOT_VOLUME
0xED.hint: The I/O system tried to mount the boot volume and failed, usually a failing boot drive or corrupted file system/boot record; CHKDSK /r and bootrec are the standard repair leads.

0xEF.name: CRITICAL_PROCESS_DIED
0xEF.hint: A process Windows can't run without (csrss.exe, wininit.exe, services.exe) died or got corrupted; if the same process is named across crashes suspect software/corruption, and run SFC /scannow.

0xF4.name: CRITICAL_OBJECT_TERMINATION
0xF4.hint: A process or thread critical to Windows died, very commonly because the OS could not read it back from a failing or disconnecting boot drive; check disk/SATA-NVMe connection and drive health first.

0xFE.name: BUGCODE_USB_DRIVER
0xFE.hint: A fatal error in the USB stack or a USB device driver; suspect the USB controller driver or a misbehaving device, and try unplugging devices or updating the USB driver.

0x101.name: CLOCK_WATCHDOG_TIMEOUT
0x101.hint: One CPU core stopped responding to clock interrupts (deadlocked or hung), typically an unstable overclock, bad CPU/voltage, or failing processor rather than a single driver.

0x102.name: DPC_WATCHDOG_TIMEOUT
0x102.hint: The DPC watchdog routine did not run in its allotted time, usually a hung driver or unresponsive hardware (often storage); same module every dump = software, varied = suspect hardware.

0x109.name: CRITICAL_STRUCTURE_CORRUPTION
0x109.hint: Something modified protected kernel code or data, which can be an incompatible driver, anti-cheat/security software, or faulty RAM; if it changes crash-to-crash run a memory test.

0x10E.name: VIDEO_MEMORY_MANAGEMENT_INTERNAL
0x10E.hint: The video memory manager (VidMm) hit an unrecoverable condition usually caused by a video driver behaving improperly, so update or cleanly reinstall the GPU driver; a consistent parameter-1 isolates the fault.

0x113.name: VIDEO_DXGKRNL_FATAL_ERROR
0x113.hint: The DirectX graphics kernel (dxgkrnl.sys) detected a fatal violation, typically triggered by the GPU driver; if the same display driver appears across crashes, a clean reinstall of the graphics driver is the usual fix.

0x116.name: VIDEO_TDR_FAILURE
0x116.hint: The GPU stopped responding and Windows' display-driver reset (TDR) failed; same faulting module across crashes (nvlddmkm=NVIDIA, amdkmdag/atikmpag=AMD, igdkmd=Intel) points to a driver/GPU fault, varied to power, heat, or overclock.

0x117.name: VIDEO_TDR_TIMEOUT_DETECTED
0x117.hint: The display driver failed to respond in time (a graphics hang) captured as a live dump; the named driver module (nvlddmkm/atikmpag) is the lead, same module repeating points to a driver, varied to cooling/power/overclock.

0x119.name: VIDEO_SCHEDULER_INTERNAL_ERROR
0x119.hint: The video scheduler caught a fatal violation, usually a misbehaving GPU driver, but parameter-1 values like 0x400/0x1000/0xA000/0x10000 flag memory corruption or bad hardware, so a stable parameter-1 says whether to update the driver or test RAM/GPU.

0x122.name: WHEA_INTERNAL_ERROR
0x122.hint: An internal failure inside the Windows Hardware Error Architecture itself, usually a buggy vendor PSHED plug-in or faulty platform/BIOS firmware; a BIOS/firmware update is the usual lead.

0x124.name: WHEA_UNCORRECTABLE_ERROR
0x124.hint: A fatal hardware error the CPU reported through WHEA, almost always failing/overheating/overclocked hardware (CPU, RAM, or motherboard); varied WHEA details across crashes suspect hardware, not software.

0x12B.name: FAULTY_HARDWARE_CORRUPTED_PAGE
0x12B.hint: A single-bit memory error hardware could not correct, pointing to defective RAM or a memory controller; run MemTest86/Windows Memory Diagnostic and reseat or swap the DIMMs.

0x133.name: DPC_WATCHDOG_VIOLATION
0x133.hint: A DPC/ISR or the system spent too long at high IRQL, classically caused by old SSD firmware or a storage/chipset driver; a recurring single driver name confirms the software lead.

0x139.name: KERNEL_SECURITY_CHECK_FAILURE
0x139.hint: The kernel caught a corrupted data structure (a security integrity check failed), usually a buggy driver; same module across crashes = software/driver, varied = suspect failing RAM.

0x141.name: VIDEO_ENGINE_TIMEOUT_DETECTED
0x141.hint: One of the GPU's display engines failed to respond in time, recorded as a live dump (LiveKernelEvent 141); the named driver module is the lead, same module repeating points to a driver, varied to overheating, power, or an unstable GPU.

0x143.name: PROCESSOR_DRIVER_INTERNAL
0x143.hint: The processor power-management driver hit an internal error, typically tied to CPU power/throttling firmware or chipset; updating BIOS and chipset/processor drivers is the lead.

0x149.name: REFS_FILE_SYSTEM
0x149.hint: A file-system error in the ReFS driver (used on Storage Spaces/data volumes), typically on-disk metadata corruption or a failing drive; check the ReFS volume and underlying disk health.

0x154.name: UNEXPECTED_STORE_EXCEPTION
0x154.hint: The kernel memory-store component hit an unexpected exception, most often a failing SSD/HDD, outdated drive firmware, or aggressive disk power settings; update SSD firmware and disable PCIe link-state power management first.

0x17E.name: MICROCODE_REVISION_MISMATCH
0x17E.hint: CPU cores ended up running different microcode versions because firmware updated only some processors; the fix is a BIOS/firmware update from the board or PC vendor.

0x1CA.name: SYNTHETIC_WATCHDOG_TIMEOUT
0x1CA.hint: A system-wide watchdog fired because the machine hung and stopped processing timer ticks; needs dump analysis to find what froze it (often a driver stuck at high IRQL or a hardware stall).

0x1DF.name: PROCESSOR_START_TIMEOUT
0x1DF.hint: A processor core failed to start in the expected time during boot or hotplug, indicating a CPU, firmware, or platform initialization problem rather than ordinary software.

0xC000021A.name: WINLOGON_FATAL_ERROR
0xC000021A.hint: A user-mode security process (Winlogon or CSRSS) died or was tampered with, so Windows shut down; commonly mismatched/corrupt system files or a bad update, so try SFC /scannow and Safe Mode.
